content:
2 min read
Just now

--

From my perspective, we train machines to learn the way we perceive the world’s information — free from emotions, focusing on an ideal approach to perceiving the information provided to them in the form of data. Just as humans reflect the people they meet, the books they read, and the movies they watch, a machine’s knowledge reflects the data it is fed. It need not be absolutely right or wrong.
That being said, it is all about the quality of the data you feed the system. Choose wisely what you spend your time with (people, books, or movies) — but remember that the quality of data alone is not sufficient to make learning ideal.
Once the quality of the data is taken care of, we should be mindful of the way we perceive it, and the choice of the appropriate model to work with the data to get the answers we seek. It doesn’t start with the data itself or the models and their benefits; it all starts with the question you want answered. What is the problem you are looking to solve?
Clarity is the key here: what do you want? Where should you look for it? And then, how do you look through it to get the answers? Isn’t it a beautiful intuition how human learning and machine learning are co-existing concepts?
So, no models are right or wrong for all situations. No one’s style of learning can be judged the same way, but the choices we make can be judged based on the outcomes we produce.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Machine learning is all about experiments, feedback, improvisation, and minimizing loss to bring about the best possible result or prediction. Our approach to life should be similar: experience your choices, analyze the output, self-correct, and improve to minimize loss. Eventually, we all grow.
